title: Evaluation of
text:
Preface
You are a data scientist for a mid-sized business, in a small group of 3-4 data scientists. You’ve been tasked with creating a report evaluating a scenario for your business. Your colleagues will also be evaluating the same scenario, and your reports will be used in aggregate to determine a consensus (or lack thereof) on the company’s action. The reports will also be used to inform downsizing that is rumored to be coming - you want to ensure your report is better than your peers so that you aren’t as easy to cut.
You may talk to your peers who are assigned the same scenario, but you do not want to collaborate too closely, lest you both become targets of the rumored layoffs.

I’ve scaffolded this report for you to make this process easier - as we talk about different sections of a report in class and read about how to create similar sections, you will practice by writing the equivalent section of your report.
The basic steps for this task are as follows:

Identify the research question from the business question
Identify data set(s) which are (1) publicly available (you don’t have a budget to pay for private data) and (2) relevant to your task

(HW Week 6) Document your data sets in draft-data-doc.qmd

Conduct a statistical analysis to support your answer to your research and business questions

Write a methods section for your business report corresponding to your statistical analysis
(HW Week 9) Draft of results section of business report with relevant graphics/visual aids in draft-results.qmd

Write your report

(HW Week 10) Draft of Intro/Conclusion sections in draft-intro-conclusions.qmd
(HW Week 11) Draft of Executive summary section in draft-exec-summary.qmd

Revise your report

(HW Week 12 – not turned in) Revise your report
(HW Week 13) - Rough draft of report due. Create one or more qmd files for your report (you can overwrite or delete intro.qmd and summary.qmd), include the names of each file (in order) in _quarto.yml. You should use references (edit references.bib and use pandoc citations). Make sure your report compiles and looks reasonable in both html and pdf.
Develop a presentation to go along with your report (Week 13). Create slides for your report using quarto.

Peer revise reports

Peer revise reports
(HW Week 14) - Make edits to your report from comments received from peer review

Final report & presentation due

title: Appendix A — Draft: Data Documentation
text:
library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

wtemp<- rbind(
  read.csv("CSV/Temp/Temp2020.csv"), 
  read.csv("CSV/Temp/Temp2022.csv"),
  read.csv("CSV/Temp/Temp2023.csv"), 
  read.csv("CSV/Temp/Temp2024.csv")
  )

watertemp<- wtemp|> group_by(Date)
wtc<- watertemp |> count(Water.Temp...F.)


Who collected the data? NOAA has stations in which they have meteorological sensors.
Why the data was collected? They collect the data for the use of analyzing sea level trends.
What the data is about? The data gives a 31 day range giving the temperatures at different points of the day.
When the data was collected.? The data is calculated throughout the year, but there missing data for specific months
Where the data was collected? The data was collected from their Dauphin Island, AL station (ID: 8735180).
How the data was generated/collected? The meteorlogical sensors collect the data by using contact based probes in intervals of 6 minutes.
Structure of the data? The data is structured where you can find temperature conductivity for each interval of time collected.
Formatting decisions in the data? They decided that for formatting you would only be able to get data in intervals of 31 days per csv file.
Data validation/quality control For validating data there are 3 algorithims that collect and process data and then are compared against satellite data.
How the data can be reused/license Due to the freedom of Information Act any person has the ritght to obtain records
Suggested data analysis methods NOAA use Optimum Interpolation Sea Surface Temperature, which uses a variety of ways that collects data and puts it in a global grid.
Measurement instruments used Meteorological Sensors

Codebook 1. Variable name in the code? Water.Temp…F. 2. Long-form description of what was measured Water temperature for the given dates 3. Units of measurement Farenheit 4. Acceptable values All temperatures measured 5. Values used to indicate missingness, refusal to respond, etc. Values have a - to indicate missingness 6. Additional notes that may be relevant This data is between July 22nd to August 22nd due to it having a higher frequency at which it would be higher than the given maximum. It may be beneficial to get more date ranges to get a scope on the rest of the year, but NOAA only allows for 31 day range per csv.

C Material and Methods
We obtained a water temperature dataset from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. The dataset had covered the hourly temperature each day from the years of 2020 to 2024 for the station in Dauphin Island. There were no availavle observations for the year of 2021. This data was used to identify the likliehood of the water temperature exceeds the temperature threshold that the plant is able to handle (> 85F).
We obtained a dataset on generation from the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The dataset had covered the overall net generation that the James M. Barry Electric Generating Plant from the years of 2020 to 2024. This dataset was used because Dauphin Island receives its power from the company Alabama Power Company, and this could be used to find the contribution a power plant would take. This was data was used to find the capabilities of the Plant in the periods where we need to run at reduced capacity (Megawatthours).
We obtained a dataset on consumption from the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The dataset had covered the consumption sales from all power companies from the years of 2020 to 2024. This dataset was used to find the consumption costs required by the power plants to meet demands (Megawatthours).
To analyze the operational risk we had calculated the proportion of temperatures that exceeded 85 F over each of the months and calculated confidence intervals for each month. We then calculated the overall impact of running at reduced risk, by finding the contribution that the power plant takes and comparing it to electrical demands.
remake following this methods section


D Results
Analysis of the water temperature from 2020 to 2024 had shown a seasonal increase in the months that occur through the middle of the year (April - October). Temperatures had peaked at around 86.6 during the months of July and August. The liklihood of exceeding 85F is high during the peak months. During the months of June and Septemember the temperature reaches near 85, but it is unlikely to exceed.

Alabama Power Company is able to have a combined capacity of 14 million Killowatts (122.72 million Megawatt-hours); James M. Barry Power Plant contributes to 27% of the maximum capacity. Alabama Power Company contributes around 40% of its electricity towards consumption. James M. Barry Power Plant contributes to around 10% of the overall consumption. James M. Barry Power Plant was able to meet the consumption for each month. The capacity when reduced is net positive over time.
